narrative: I'm sorry to deliver this news, but the law school's admission model predicted a 56.7% chance that you would fail the bar exam on your first try, which is why your application was rejected—they use this prediction to prioritize students likely to succeed. Your profile includes a Year 1 school decile of 3, which is below the typical student's 5.71; a Year 3 decile of 1, much lower than the average 5.53; an undergraduate GPA of 2.90, under the dataset average of 3.21; and an LSAT score of 37, just slightly above the average 36.70. You're from a middle-income family, identify as male and White—both common in the applicant pool—and plan to attend full-time, like most students. The model's decision was driven most strongly by your Year 3 academic decile, which had the biggest influence in pushing the prediction toward failure, as your very low ranking there signaled potential struggles ahead. Next in importance was your Year 1 decile, which also contributed toward the failure prediction by being below average. Your LSAT score came third, surprisingly nudging the odds toward failure despite being a bit above average, perhaps because it wasn't standout enough to offset other weaknesses. Family income ranked fourth and likewise pushed in the failure direction for your middle bracket. Your undergraduate GPA was fifth most influential, with its below-average level adding to the risk assessment. Less impactful but still contributing were your racial background, gender, and full-time status, all tilting slightly toward the failure prediction in your case. Compared to typical students who pass the bar, your academic deciles and GPA stand out as notably weaker, while your LSAT and background align closely with averages. To improve your future chances, focus on building stronger study habits and academic performance now, perhaps through targeted prep courses or tutoring to address the patterns seen in your school deciles. Retaking the LSAT for a higher score could help too, as even a modest boost might shift predictions favorably. With dedication to these areas, you can demonstrate growth and boost your bar exam readiness for another application down the line. Keep pushing—you have solid foundations in other areas to build on.
